target.title: Feature/issue: 71428 manifest conversation access
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

  - Problem: non-bundled plugins could not declare that they need raw conversation-content typed hooks in their manifest, leaving the requirement only in host config.
  - Why it matters: plugin authors need a manifest-level way to advertise sensitive conversation access needs, while operators must still explicitly approve that access.
  - What changed: added `permissions.conversationAccess` parsing/preservation for plugin manifests, documented it as a request, updated registry records/tests, and kept effective access gated by `plugins.entries.<id>.hooks.allowConversationAccess=true`.
  - What did NOT change (scope boundary): manifest permission does not grant conversation access by itself; no new consent flow, installer behavior, or runtime hook surface was added.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #71428
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

  - Root cause: manifest loading did not model plugin-declared conversation access requirements, so the loader/registry had no structured request metadata to preserve.
  - Missing detection / guardrail: manifest registry tests did not cover sensitive permission metadata.
  - Contributing context (if known): conversation-content hooks already required explicit host trust, but manifest metadata had no matching request field.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
  - Target test or file: `src/plugins/manifest-registry.test.ts`, `src/plugins/loader.test.ts`
  - Scenario the test should lock in: manifests preserve `permissions.conversationAccess: true`, ignore absent/non-true values, and do not grant non-bundled conversation hooks without explicit host config.
  - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: manifest parsing and loader hook policy are the exact seams involved.
  - Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing loader tests covered explicit `allowConversationAccess=true`; new tests cover manifest request metadata and no implicit grant.
  - If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

  {
    "permissions": {
      "conversationAccess": true
    }
  }

This declares a request only. Operators must still set plugins.entries.<id>.hooks.allowConversationAccess=true to grant raw conversation hook access.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
  Before:
  [plugin manifest] -> [no conversation access metadata] -> [host config only]

  After:
  [plugin manifest request] -> [registry preserves request]
  [host config approval] -> [conversation hook access granted]
```

## Security Impact (required)

  - New permissions/capabilities? (Yes)
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
  - New/changed network calls? (No)
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
  - Data access scope changed? (No)
  - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: adds a manifest-declared permission request, but does not grant access. Raw conversation hooks remain gated by explicit host config.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

  - OS: macOS
  - Runtime/container: local repo, Node/pnpm
  - Model/provider: N/A
  - Integration/channel (if any): plugin runtime hooks

## Summary

Implements **EV-002B** (Skill Workshop defense-in-depth) per Evolves plan: gated procedural writer stays **only** in `extensions/skill-workshop`; no core `skills_manage` tool.

### Changes
- **G2 path:** `isPathInside` / `isPathInsideWithRealpath` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/security-runtime` (re-export from `scan-paths`) for skill dir, SKILL.md, and support files.
- **G3 TOCTOU:** Second `prepareProposalWrite` immediately before `atomicWrite`; stale workspace throws `skill proposal stale: workspace changed before write`.
- **G4 economics:** `enforceSkillsPromptBudgetIfConfigured` uses `previewSkillsPromptImpact` / `buildSyntheticWorkspaceSkillEntryForPreview` (exported via `skills-runtime`) so queue and apply align with core skills prompt limits.
- **Docs:** `docs/plugins/skill-workshop.md` — security model, memory vs Workshop, maintenance grep note.
- **Tests:** pending no disk write, quarantine no SKILL.md, replace drift, POSIX symlink escape (`it.skipIf(win32)`).

### Not in this PR

target.body:
## What

Add comprehensive test coverage for `extensions/diffs/src/viewer-client.ts` (361 lines, previously only bundle-content grep coverage). Covers four untested code paths:

1. **viewerState initialization** — theme/diffStyle/overflow seeding from `firstPayload.options`, default values when payload uses defaults, and `preloadHighlighter` language merging across multiple cards
2. **toolbar button toggles** — layout (unified↔split) and theme (light↔dark) toggle state changes, multi-card sync
3. **ensureShadowRoot** — shadow root attachment from declarative `<template>`, skip when no template present, skip when already attached
4. **getHydrateProps branching** — `fileDiff` vs `oldFile/newFile` payload shape dispatch

## Why

`viewer-client.ts` owns the entire client-side diff viewer: payload parsing, shadow-root attachment, toolbar construction, theme/layout toggle state, and multi-card synchronization. The only prior test coverage was a bundle-content grep in `config.test.ts` checking for three minified style string literals (line 383-394).

## How

- Extended `renderCard()` helper with optional `payloadOverride` parameter for custom payload injection
- Added 17 new test cases across 4 `describe` blocks using the existing `@pierre/diffs` mock setup
- All tests use jsdom environment and the existing `vi.hoisted()` mock pattern

Fixes #83915

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Test coverage for viewer-client.ts initialization, toolbar toggles, shadow root attachment, and hydrateProps branching

target.title: fix(gateway): unblock startup and fresh agent sessions
target.body:
## Summary

- rebase the patch onto current `main`
- allow `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_FAST_CONFIG=1` to defer pre-ready channel startup maintenance and startup session migration
- keep deferred failures visible by warning on rejected background startup tasks
- add regression coverage for fast mode and normal mode behavior

## Testing

- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm check`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/gateway/server-startup-plugins.test.ts`
- `env -u OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/gateway/server-runtime-config.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-config.secrets.test.ts`
- `pnpm test` *(repo-wide current-main failures still exist outside this diff; in this environment the first gateway auth failures were caused by a pre-set `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN`, and later unrelated failures were in untouched files including `src/config/sessions/transcript.test.ts`, `src/config/io.compat.test.ts`, `src/plugins/bundled-plugin-metadata.test.ts`, and `extensions/opencode-go/index.test.ts`)*

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Allow `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_FAST_CONFIG=1` to defer pre-ready channel startup maintenance and startup session migration without swallowing deferred failures, while preserving the existing blocking behavior in normal mode.

**Real environment tested**:
- local PR branch rebased onto current `origin/main`
- foreground OpenClaw dev gateway run from this branch on Linux
- gateway started with `--auth none --port 19041`

**Exact steps or command run after the patch**:
```bash
OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_FAST_CONFIG=1 node openclaw.mjs --dev gateway run --allow-unconfigured --auth none --port 19041 --verbose
curl --silent http://127.0.0.1:19041/health
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```text
15:43:23 [gateway] starting HTTP server...
15:43:24 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: acpx, browser, device-pair, discord, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 9.2s)
15:43:27 [gateway] ready
```

```json
{"ok":true,"status":"live"}
```

**Observed result after fix**:
- with `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_FAST_CONFIG=1`, the gateway reached `ready` and answered `/health` from a real foreground run on this branch
- fast mode no longer blocks readiness on startup maintenance/session migration before plugin loading completes
- the deferred failure path now warns instead of silently swallowing a rejected background startup task
- normal mode still waits for both startup tasks before plugin loading continues

**What was not tested**:
- a live forced failure of channel-maintenance or session-migration work during the foreground gateway run
- production-scale startup/session-migration workloads
- every channel plugin's startup-maintenance implementation

## AI assistance

- AI-assisted: GitHub Copilot CLI
- I reviewed the final patch and understand the changed startup path
- I resolved the applicable bot-review findings that were in scope for the final diff
- I attempted `codex review --base origin/main`, but local Codex auth in this environment was expired (`refresh_token_reused`), so there is no local Codex artifact to attach

target.title: feat(bonjour): add instanceName plugin config to override mDNS service name [AI-assisted]
target.body:
Closes #54467.

## Summary

Adds a `plugins.entries.bonjour.config.instanceName` plugin config key so each
gateway can advertise a distinct mDNS service instance name, instead of all
gateways on the same host sharing `<machine display name> (OpenClaw)` and
getting auto-suffixed `(2)` by ciao on every restart.

The advertiser already accepted an `opts.instanceName` override
(`extensions/bonjour/src/advertiser.ts:201-205`); only the plugin entry was
missing the config-driven path. Behavior with no config set is unchanged.

## Why this fits the plugin pattern (not `gateway.bonjour.*`)

The original issue suggested `gateway.bonjour.instanceName`, but per the
plugins boundary rules in `src/plugins/CLAUDE.md` ("do not normalize
plugin-owned into core-owned by scattering direct reads of
`plugins.entries.<id>.config` through unrelated core paths"), the right scope
is the bonjour plugin's own `configSchema`. Reads happen in
`extensions/bonjour/index.ts` via `api.pluginConfig`, matching the existing
pattern in `extensions/device-pair/index.ts:299`.

## Behavior

- `plugins.entries.bonjour.config.instanceName` unset → unchanged: uses
  `ctx.machineDisplayName`, suffixed `(OpenClaw)`.
- Set to `"AI VA PC"` → advertises as `AI VA PC (OpenClaw)`.
- Set to `"My Bot (OpenClaw)"` → advertises as `My Bot (OpenClaw)` (no
  double-suffix; existing `formatBonjourInstanceName` regex already handles
  this).
- Set to empty / whitespace → falls back to machine name.
- Non-string value → falls back to machine name (defensive).

## Repro (per #54467)

Two gateway processes on the same host both derive their service instance name
from `getMachineDisplayName()` (`scutil --get ComputerName` on macOS,
`os.hostname()` elsewhere). Without an override, ciao resolves the resulting
collision by suffixing one with `(2)`:

```
+   eth0 IPv4 mac-mini (OpenClaw)         _openclaw-gw._tcp    local
+   eth0 IPv4 mac-mini (OpenClaw) (2)     _openclaw-gw._tcp    local   ← ciao auto-rename
```

The reporter notes this can shift between instances on restart. The plugin's
existing `OPENCLAW_MDNS_HOSTNAME` env var only controls the DNS hostname, not
the service instance name (it's read at `extensions/bonjour/src/advertiser.ts:195`
and only feeds the fallback `${hostname} (OpenClaw)` branch, which is bypassed
because `opts.instanceName` is always non-empty after `formatBonjourInstanceName`).

## After

```jsonc
// gateway A's openclaw.json
{
  "plugins": {
    "entries": {
      "bonjour": {
        "config": { "instanceName": "Mac Mini Primary" }
      }
    }
  }
}

// gateway B's openclaw.json
{
  "plugins": {
    "entries": {
      "bonjour": {
        "config": { "instanceName": "Mac Mini Bot" }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

```
+   eth0 IPv4 Mac Mini Primary (OpenClaw)   _openclaw-gw._tcp    local
+   eth0 IPv4 Mac Mini Bot (OpenClaw)       _openclaw-gw._tcp    local
```

Each gateway is stably discoverable on its own name across restarts.

## Files

- `extensions/bonjour/openclaw.plugin.json` — add `instanceName` to
  `configSchema.properties`.
- `extensions/bonjour/index.ts` — read `api.pluginConfig.instanceName`,
  prefer it over `ctx.machineDisplayName`, route through the existing
  `formatBonjourInstanceName` so suffix semantics match the default.
- `extensions/bonjour/index.test.ts` — new plugin-entry test (5 cases:
  unset, empty/whitespace, configured-without-suffix, configured-with-suffix,
  defensive non-string).
- `docs/gateway/bonjour.md` — document the new config key alongside the
  existing disable/configure entries.
- `CHANGELOG.md` — single-line entry under `## 2026.4.25 (Unreleased)`.

## Tests

- `pnpm test extensions/bonjour` → 32/32 pass (4 files, includes the new 5).
- `pnpm check:changed` → green (typecheck + format + tests for affected lanes).
- `pnpm build` → ok.

## AI-assisted disclosure

This PR was authored with Claude (Anthropic). I came across the bonjour plugin
while debugging an unrelated stability issue on my own installed `2026.4.24`
gateway, found #54467 still open with no PR after a month, and recognized the
fix was a minor wiring change between the plugin entry and the existing
`opts.instanceName` advertiser path. Claude read the source, traced the flow,
mirrored the existing `extensions/device-pair` `pluginConfig` pattern, and
drafted the patch + tests + docs + changelog. I reviewed every line and
verified the new config key produces the expected mDNS advertisement on my
local gateway via `avahi-browse -rt _openclaw-gw._tcp`.

Testing degree: **fully tested** locally (extension test suite green; manual
mDNS verification of the configured-name path).

target.title: fix #92688: [Bug]: Qwen vision models fail with 400 "Unexpected item type in content" on DashScope
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

Root cause: OpenClaw's media-understanding resolver trusted user/catalog `input: ["text", "image"]` metadata as the final image-capability source of truth. That allowed a known text-only Qwen Cloud chat model such as `qwen3.7-max` to cross the image resolver contract and be routed into DashScope image requests, which caused `400 Unexpected item type in content` instead of selecting the provider's image-capable default.

Why does this matter now?

A bad routing decision loses the user's image-understanding request before the Qwen provider can return a useful result. The issue is user-visible because an otherwise valid Qwen setup can fail whenever a text-only max chat model is configured with overly broad image metadata.

What is the intended outcome?

Fix classification: root-cause fix. Provider-owned media-understanding metadata is now the canonical contract for model capability overrides, and Qwen-specific defaults/capability facts live in the Qwen plugin runtime/manifest instead of shared core production logic.

What is intentionally out of scope?

What did NOT change: this does not change Qwen auth, endpoint selection, API-key handling, request transport, or live DashScope behavior. It intentionally does not upgrade the Qwen image default to `qwen3.5-plus`; the default remains `qwen-vl-max-latest`. No unrelated provider defaults or migration behavior were changed.

What does success look like?

A configured text-only model like `qwen3.7-max` is rejected as an image model when provider-owned metadata marks it non-image, and OpenClaw falls back to the Qwen image default `qwen-vl-max-latest` instead of sending that chat model through the image path.

What should reviewers focus on?

Architecture / source-of-truth check: shared core only consumes the generic `modelCapabilityOverrides` contract. Qwen-specific provider aliases, default models, and `qwen3.7-max` non-image family facts are owned by the Qwen plugin and manifest metadata. This keeps the canonical provider contract at the plugin seam and prevents future Qwen capability drift from hard-coded shared-core mappings.

## Linked context

Closes #92688.

Related open PR scan: this refresh is scoped to #92782 / #92688 and the Qwen media-understanding contract. I did not find a reason to fold in any unrelated open PR behavior or broaden the public contract beyond provider-owned model capability overrides.

This refresh addresses the author-side review finding on #92782: Qwen-specific capability/default facts should be owned by the Qwen plugin/manifest, not hard-coded in shared core.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: a Qwen text-only chat model such as `qwen3.7-max` can be configured with `input: ["text", "image"]`, but should not be routed into the DashScope image request path when provider-owned metadata says the model is non-image.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw workspace using the real media-understanding resolver, provider registry, manifest metadata registry, image tool/PDF tool config paths, and Qwen plugin registration path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/test-projects.mjs \
  src/plugins/manifest-registry.test.ts \
  src/media-understanding/defaults.test.ts \
  src/media-understanding/provider-registry.test.ts \
  src/media-understanding/provider-capability-registry.test.ts \
  src/media-understanding/runner.vision-skip.test.ts \
  src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts \
  src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.model-config.test.ts \
  src/media-understanding/image.test.ts \
  extensions/qwen/index.test.ts \
  extensions/qwen/media-understanding-provider.test.ts \
  --testNamePattern "mediaUnderstandingProviderMetadata|bundled image defaults|bundled video fallback|Qwen text-only|provider-owned metadata|plugin-owned model overrides|configured Qwen max chat|Qwen max chat metadata|known non-image Qwen|media-understanding aliases|qwen video|Qwen video|Qwen Cloud media"
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.media-understanding.config.ts

      Tests  1 passed | 8 skipped (9)

[test] passed 4 Vitest shards in 54.25s
```

- Observed result after fix: resolver coverage now rejects provider-owned text-only image metadata and falls back to the provider image default. The Qwen plugin registers `qwen`, `qwencloud`, `modelstudio`, and `dashscope` media-understanding providers with `qwen-vl-max-latest` as the image default and `qwen3.7-max` as a non-image family.
- What was not tested: no live DashScope API request was rerun in this patch refresh.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this environment does not include a live Qwen/DashScope API key, so the proof here validates the OpenClaw routing decision that prevents the bad DashScope image request rather than calling DashScope itself.
- Before evidence: the RED test for provider-owned metadata previously selected the configured text-only model (`atlas-chat`) as an image model instead of falling back to the provider image default. The Qwen alias registration RED test also showed only `qwen` registered before this refresh; the aliases are now registered with provider-owned metadata.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

Target test file: focused regression coverage was run through `node scripts/test-projects.mjs` across media-understanding, agents tool config, plugin manifest, and Qwen extension provider test shards. After remote CI exposed the missing registration-contract expectation, the failing contract was reproduced locally and the full contracts-plugin shard was rerun. Type coverage was also run with `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:extensions`.

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-registration.contract.test.ts -t "qwen plugin registration contract"
```

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  3 passed | 87 skipped (90)
```

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts
```

```text
Test Files  43 passed (43)
     Tests  953 passed (953)
```

```bash
pnpm tsgo:core
```

```text
$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo
```

```bash
pnpm tsgo:extensions
```

```text
$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

Scenario locked in: configured text-only model metadata is no longer enough to make a model image-capable when provider-owned metadata says otherwise. Coverage also locks that Qwen aliases receive the same plugin-owned metadata and that only canonical `qwen` contributes video auto-priority, preserving the default video fallback order.

What failed before this fix, if known?

Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the first provider-owned metadata RED test selected the configured text-only model (`atlas-chat`) instead of the provider image default (`atlas-vision`). The Qwen alias registration RED test showed that only `qwen` was registered before this refresh. These targeted tests cover the routing contract directly without requiring a live API key.

If no test was added, why not?

Focused tests were added/updated for the resolver, registries, manifest metadata, image/PDF tool paths, and Qwen plugin registration.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`

What is the highest-risk area?

Risk labels considered: compatibility and auth-provider. The highest-risk area is the public config/provider model-selection contract, because this patch changes how configured `input` metadata is interpreted when provider-owned capability overrides exist.

Risk explanation: the compatibility risk is intentional and narrow: a model configured as image-capable can now be ignored for image routing only when the provider's own metadata marks it non-image. The auth-provider risk is limited to provider metadata registration; this patch does not change tokens, credentials, endpoint classes, request headers, or API-key resolution.

How is that risk mitigated?

Why acceptable: the generic contract makes provider-owned metadata the canonical boundary, keeps Qwen-specific facts out of shared core, and preserves the existing Qwen default `qwen-vl-max-latest`. Patch quality warnings around fallback/default language are not masking the symptom; they describe the intended resolver behavior after the source-of-truth contract rejects a text-only model.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Maintainer-ready confidence: high after focused resolver/plugin validation and type checks. Next action is maintainer review and CI on the refreshed head.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

The old `status: waiting on author` label maps to the previous patch shape. Author-side repair is complete in this refresh; remaining work is maintainer/CI review after submit. Live DashScope proof was not rerun because no Qwen/DashScope key is available in this environment.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- RF-001 fixed/disclosed: the `status: waiting on author` label was caused by patch-shape findings; this refresh addresses those author-side findings and is ready for maintainer/CI review.
- RF-002 fixed: failed CI checks are refreshed through local focused validation and type checks; remote CI should rerun on the submitted head.
- RF-003 fixed: shared core no longer encodes Qwen provider aliases or `qwen3.7-max` facts; it consumes generic provider-owned `modelCapabilityOverrides`.
- RF-004 fixed: the Qwen image default remains `qwen-vl-max-latest`; this patch does not switch the default to `qwen3.5-plus`.
- RF-005 fixed locally: core and extension type checks pass on the current patch, and focused tests pass on the updated routing contract.
- RF-006 resolved for review: the provider capability seam is now a generic provider-owned contract, and the default-model change concern is removed by keeping `qwen-vl-max-latest`.
- RF-007 fixed: Qwen model facts are moved out of shared core production logic and into Qwen plugin runtime/manifest metadata.
- RF-008 fixed: Qwen image default is not changed without upgrade proof; it remains `qwen-vl-max-latest`.
- RF-009 fixed: runner image-skip behavior is covered in the focused media-understanding shard.
- RF-010 fixed: image tool and PDF tool configured-model paths are covered in the focused agents shard.
- RF-011 fixed: image/defaults/Qwen plugin coverage is included in the focused media-understanding and extension-provider shards.
- RF-012 fixed locally: final-head validation has been refreshed locally; remote CI should rerun after submit.

Patch quality notes: the diff touches multiple resolver entry points because the same source-of-truth boundary is consumed by defaults, runner selection, image runtime, image tool config, PDF tool config, provider registry, capability registry, and manifest metadata. Splitting those consumers would leave some user paths still able to route text-only models into image requests.

target.title: fix(whatsapp): extract GIF metadata and distinguish gifPlayback in media placeholders (fixes #49099)
target.body:
## Summary

- Distinguish GIFs from regular videos using `videoMessage.gifPlayback` (`<media:gif>` vs `<media:video>`)
- Extract `title`, `sourceUrl`, and `body` from `contextInfo.externalAdReply` on video and image messages
- Add `escapeAttr` helper to sanitize `"` and `>` in attribute values
- Enables the bot to understand GIF content from Giphy/Tenor URLs without requiring ffmpeg or video processing

Before: `<media:video>`
After: `<media:gif title="This Is Fine" source="https://giphy.com/gifs/this-is-fine-3o7TK">`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** WhatsApp GIF metadata extraction — `extractMediaPlaceholder` now checks `gifPlayback` to return `<media:gif>` vs `<media:video>`, and extracts `title`/`sourceUrl`/`body` from `contextInfo.externalAdReply` on video and image messages
- **Environment tested:** macOS, Node.js, pnpm monorepo build
- **Steps run after the patch:**
  1. Ran targeted tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound.test.ts`
  2. Ran extract tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/extract.ts`

target.title: fix(codex): preserve semantic native threads across compaction
target.body:
## Summary

This is PR #4 in the Codex native-thread stack, after #85978, #86069, and #86094. It turns the diagnostics foundation into the long-term cache/lifecycle fix for compatible context-engine `thread_bootstrap` bindings:

- preserve Codex `thread_bootstrap` bindings across successful context-engine-owned compaction;
- copy preserved bindings to successor session files during transcript rollover, then clear the archived/original binding;
- reread the active Codex binding after forced compaction before rebuilding the Codex prompt;
- if optimistic semantic reuse rotates during startup, rebuild the prompt for the fresh thread before `turn/start` so the fresh thread receives projected context;
- keep legacy, ownerless, non-bootstrap, mismatch, and app-server rejection paths rotating/clearing as before;
- hash sensitive MCP/environment comparison tokens before persisting or emitting lifecycle diagnostics;
- add `context-engine-compaction-preserved-binding` lifecycle diagnostics and docs explaining the preserved vs invalidated paths.

## Why this is not “raise 70k to 258k/272k”

The default `70000` native reuse guard remains a legacy/stale-binding safety rail. The healthy Codex + LCM path is semantic ownership:

1. Context engine returns `contextProjection.mode = "thread_bootstrap"` with a stable epoch/fingerprint.
2. OpenClaw injects large assembled context once into the Codex native thread.
3. Later turns resume that native thread and send only the current prompt when the engine/policy/projection/tool identity still matches.
4. Compaction or rollover preserves the binding only when that semantic identity remains safe.
5. Actual app-server overflow/rejection still clears the binding before fresh-thread retry.

The important distinction is model capacity vs warm-thread lifecycle policy:

| Value | Meaning | Share of 70k guard |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `70000` | OpenClaw proactive native-thread reuse guard for legacy/stale bindings | 100% |
| `272000` | OpenClaw Codex runtime input cap used for GPT-5.5 forward-compat rows | 70k is 25.7% |
| `400000` | GPT-5.5 native/context window in current OpenClaw catalog/test fixtures | 70k is 17.5% |

With a 30% post-compaction target, even a compacted transcript can land above the guard:

- 30% of 258k = 77.4k
- 30% of 272k = 81.6k

So repeatedly applying a hard 70k warm-thread rotation rule to otherwise compatible Codex native threads makes long-running sessions take the cold path right after compaction. This PR keeps the 70k guard for legacy/non-semantic bindings, but lets semantically-owned `thread_bootstrap` bindings survive compaction/rollover.

## Local bootstrap size scan

I ran a bounded local scan over named bootstrap candidates (`AGENTS.md`, `USER.md`, `MEMORY.md`, `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `BOOTSTRAP.md`) under `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos`, `/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex`, `~/.openclaw`, and `~/.codex`, using `ceil(bytes / 4)` as a conservative token estimate.

| Metric | Result |
| --- | ---: |
| Candidate files | 3,235 |
| Aggregate directories | 2,032 |
| Aggregate p50 | ~637 tokens |
| Aggregate p90 | ~3.8k tokens |
| Aggregate p99 | ~13.5k tokens |
| Max aggregate | ~117.7k tokens (`~/.codex/memories/MEMORY.md`) |
| Largest repeated AGENTS candidate | ~23.6k tokens |

Interpretation: the bug is not “every AGENTS.md is over 70k.” The failure mode is total rendered pressure: compacted history + context-engine projection + workspace bootstrap/developer instructions + native rollout growth. Once that total has created a warm native thread, the efficient Codex path is to preserve the semantically valid native thread rather than reproject the large stable payload every turn.

## Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Long-running Codex session"] --> B{"Saved native binding?"}
  B -- "Legacy / ownerless / non-bootstrap" --> C{"session/native tokens >= guard?"}
  C -- "yes" --> D["Rotate cold: thread/start + reproject context"]
  C -- "no" --> E["Try normal resume"]
  B -- "context-engine thread_bootstrap" --> F{"Engine id + policy + epoch/fingerprint + tools match?"}
  F -- "no" --> D
  F -- "yes" --> G["Resume warm native thread"]
  G --> H["Send current prompt only"]
  H --> I{"Context-engine compaction?"}
  I -- "same file, identity stable" --> J["Preserve binding in place"]
  I -- "successor rollover, identity stable" --> K["Copy binding to successor; clear archived original"]
  I -- "identity changes or app-server rejects" --> D
  J --> G
  K --> G
```

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Compatible Codex context-engine `thread_bootstrap` native bindings are preserved across successful context-engine compaction and successor session-file rollover, while legacy/non-bootstrap, mismatch, and app-server rejection paths still rotate/clear.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw worktree on the Lexar-backed checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/worktrees/openclaw-codex-semantic-reuse-guard`, branch `codex/semantic-native-thread-preservation`, current head `8b405b4d5c59432b4f3218b570609d3d43f5a4ad`. The proof used temp session files under `/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/openclaw-pr-86160-proof-8b405b4d5c` and the actual exported `reconcileContextEngineCompactedCodexBinding(...)`, `writeCodexAppServerBinding(...)`, `readCodexAppServerBinding(...)`, `clearCodexAppServerBinding(...)`, and `startOrResumeThread(...)` helpers from this checkout.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a one-off `pnpm exec tsx` proof probe, not Vitest/tsgo/build. The probe wrote matching `thread_bootstrap` bindings, simulated a compactor clearing the sidecar before same-file compaction, simulated successor rollover copy/archived clear, and seeded a legacy raw user-MCP binding to prove the mismatch path rotates through `thread/start` and persists a hashed comparison token. I also verified the patched worktree identity and whitespace state with `pwd`, `git rev-parse HEAD`, `git branch --show-current`, `git status --porcelain`, and `git diff --check HEAD^ HEAD`.
- Evidence after fix: Copied terminal output from the current patched worktree proof probe:

```text
[agent/embedded] context-engine-owned Codex app-server compaction preserved native thread-bootstrap binding
[agent/embedded] context-engine-owned Codex app-server compaction preserved native thread-bootstrap binding
{
  "head": "8b405b4d5c59432b4f3218b570609d3d43f5a4ad",
  "proofDir": "/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/openclaw-pr-86160-proof-8b405b4d5c",
  "sameFileThreadBootstrap": {
    "outcome": {
      "invalidated": false,
      "preserved": true,
      "successorInvalidated": false
    },
    "restoredAfterCompactorClearedSidecar": true,
    "preservedThreadId": "thread-same-file-warm",
    "projectionMode": "thread_bootstrap",
    "projectionEpoch": "epoch-proof",
    "projectionFingerprint": "projection-fp-proof"
  },
  "successorRolloverThreadBootstrap": {
    "outcome": {
      "invalidated": false,
      "preserved": true,
      "successorInvalidated": false
    },
    "archivedBindingPresent": false,
    "successorThreadId": "thread-rollover-warm",
    "successorSessionFileBasename": "rollover-successor.jsonl",
    "projectionMode": "thread_bootstrap",
    "projectionEpoch": "epoch-rollover",
    "projectionFingerprint": "projection-fp-rollover"
  },
  "mcpConfigMismatchRotation": {
    "lifecycleAction": "started",
    "methods": [
      "thread/start"
    ],
    "previousThreadRotated": true,
    "savedThreadId": "thread-mcp-new",
    "userMcpServersFingerprintPrefix": "sha256:",
    "leakedMcpSecretInBinding": false,
    "leakedMcpPathInBinding": false
  }
}
$ pwd
/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/worktrees/openclaw-codex-semantic-reuse-guard
$ git rev-parse HEAD
8b405b4d5c59432b4f3218b570609d3d43f5a4ad
$ git branch --show-current
codex/semantic-native-thread-preservation
$ git status --porcelain
$ git diff --check HEAD^ HEAD
```

`git status --porcelain` and `git diff --check HEAD^ HEAD` produced no output.

- Observed result after fix: Same-file compaction returned `preserved: true` and restored `thread-same-file-warm` after the sidecar was cleared. Successor rollover returned `preserved: true`, cleared the archived/original binding (`archivedBindingPresent: false`), and copied `thread-rollover-warm` with the same `thread_bootstrap` epoch/fingerprint to the successor session file. The MCP mismatch path did not resume the stale thread; it called `thread/start`, saved `thread-mcp-new`, persisted a `sha256:` comparison fingerprint, and did not leak the seeded MCP email/path into the saved binding.
- What was not tested: No local Vitest/tsgo suite, package build, or live Gateway/Discord/Codex app-server replay was run locally, to avoid the local resource issue called out in AGENTS.md. GitHub Actions remains the validation gate for type, package-boundary, and test execution.
- Before evidence: The motivating production-style logs reported repeated `codex app-server native transcript exceeded active token limit; starting a fresh thread` at native token counts above the 70k reuse guard; issue #86023 and this PR body include the budget math and local bootstrap-size scan explaining that failure mode.

## Validation

Local validation intentionally skipped heavy suites per AGENTS.md / user instruction. GitHub Actions is the validation gate.

Local:

- one-off `pnpm exec tsx` lifecycle proof probe, as captured above
- `git diff --check HEAD^ HEAD`

GitHub Actions:

- current head `8b405b4d5c59432b4f3218b570609d3d43f5a4ad` is green, including the previously failing logging support-export slice after the stability-bundle `mode` parser fix and the rerun after the unrelated model-catalog timeout.

Not run locally:

- Vitest focused slices
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- live Gateway/Discord/Codex app-server replay

## Stack / Review Notes

This PR is stacked on #86094. The diff to review is the top commit:

- `8b405b4d5c fix(codex): preserve semantic native threads across compaction`

Refs #86023.

target.title: feat: add external plugin approval verification
target.body:
## Summary

This is the narrowed AgentKit host API slice after @kevinslin's feedback about avoiding a broad custom-action surface.

- Adds `externalResolution` for plugin approvals as a single command-template field, not arbitrary custom action arrays.
- Keeps core as the normal approval owner: OpenClaw still renders core decisions from `allowedDecisions`, and AgentKit uses only core `deny` there.
- Adds `plugin.approval.resolveVerified` for proof-backed allow resolution, scoped to the originating `pluginId` and registered behind `operator.admin`.
- Teaches approval view models, text/channel renderers, Control UI parsing, protocol schema, docs, and SDK types to carry/render the narrow external verification command.
- Rejects mixed normal allow decisions with external verification, so allow-once/allow-always must go through the verified plugin-owned resolver.

This replaces the earlier broader approach from #82431. The chat/status-card follow-up from #82471 is not required for the current AgentKit end-to-end path.

## Review context

This PR is the narrower replacement for the earlier custom-action approach. It does **not** add arbitrary plugin-defined approval actions.

The concrete use case is the external AgentKit plugin:

- https://github.com/Guardiola31337/openclaw-agentkit
- https://github.com/Guardiola31337/openclaw-agentkit/pull/2
- https://clawhub.ai/plugins/@guardiola31337/agentkit

AgentKit needs to let a user complete external World verification before a protected OpenClaw tool continues, while keeping OpenClaw core as the approval owner.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A[Agent calls protected tool] --> B[OpenClaw creates plugin approval]
  B --> C[OpenClaw renders normal approval prompt]
  C --> D[User chooses external verification command]
  D --> E[AgentKit starts World verification]
  E --> F[World proof succeeds]
  F --> G[AgentKit calls resolveVerified]
  G --> H[OpenClaw checks plugin ownership]
  H --> I[Tool call continues]
```

## Trust boundary

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph Core[OpenClaw core]
    A[Owns pending approval]
    B[Renders normal deny and timeout handling]
    C[Validates approval id]
    D[Checks originating plugin id]
    E[Resolves approval]
  end

  subgraph Plugin[AgentKit plugin]
    F[Owns World verification]
    G[Shows QR/link]
    H[Submits proof-backed allow]
  end

  A --> F
  H --> D
  D --> E
```

## Why this shape

The earlier approach exposed arbitrary `actions`, which made every approval-capable client understand plugin-defined buttons. That was too broad for this use case.

This PR keeps the core approval contract intact and adds only:

- `externalResolution`: a narrow command-template hint for external verification.
- `plugin.approval.resolveVerified`: an ownership-checked resolver so plugin X can resolve only approvals created by plugin X.

Core still owns pending approval state, timeout, deny handling, and normal approval rendering. AgentKit owns only the external World verification step and can only resolve approvals that originated from AgentKit.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-approval.test.ts src/gateway/method-scopes.test.ts src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/approval-renderers.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts src/infra/approval-view-model.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/exec-approval.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts extensions/slack/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts extensions/discord/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts extensions/matrix/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/build-all.mjs`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.plugin-sdk.dts.json --declaration true`
- `node --max-old-space-size=8192 --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: external HITL plugins can ask OpenClaw to show a narrow verification command for allow decisions while core keeps denial and pending approval ownership.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Node 22 using the gateway/plugin approval tests, channel renderer tests, Control UI tests, full build, SDK API check, and a companion AgentKit plugin gateway e2e run against this host API.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the commands listed in `Verification`, plus `pnpm test:openclaw-hitl` in the companion AgentKit plugin checkout linked to this OpenClaw host API branch.

Evidence after fix: copied after-fix output from the AgentKit local OpenClaw gateway e2e run:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
```

Observed result after fix: plugin approval prompts render external verification as command text, core deny remains available, verified allow resolution is admin-scoped and plugin-owned, and reaction/manual fallback prompts no longer claim `allow-always` is unavailable when external verification exposes it.

target.title: fix(models): avoid persisting prompt-visible provider secrets
target.body:
## Summary
- stop newly generated plaintext provider apiKey values from being written to models.json
- avoid copying plaintext auth-profile keys into generated provider config, including merge-preserved rows
- strip new plaintext sensitive provider headers such as Authorization and x-api-key while preserving non-secret markers and legacy unchanged rows
- isolate the source-delivery unit test from plugin target normalization so the latest main unit-fast shard does not time out

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Provider credentials that are needed for runtime auth should not be duplicated into the prompt-visible models.json catalog when they are new plaintext apiKey values or sensitive header values.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows 11, Node v24.13.0, pnpm 11.1.0, running the patched source through tsx against a real config-shaped provider entry.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a tsx script that calls the patched models.json planner with a custom provider containing plaintext apiKey, Authorization, and X-Api-Key values plus an OpenAI provider using non-secret markers.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output from that local run:

```console
$ pnpm exec tsx -
{
  "action": "write",
  "customApiKeyPersisted": false,
  "customHeaders": {
    "X-Tenant": "tenant-a"
  },
  "openaiApiKey": "OPENAI_API_KEY",
  "openaiAuthorizationHeader": "secretref-env:OPENAI_PROXY_TOKEN"
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The new plaintext provider apiKey was absent, the plaintext Authorization and X-Api-Key headers were removed, the safe X-Tenant header remained, and the non-secret env/SecretRef markers were preserved.

**What was not tested:** Full live provider request execution was not re-run; this PR only proves the persisted models.json planning path.

## Validation
- corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/config.shared-auth.test.ts
- corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/models-config.applies-config-env-vars.test.ts src/agents/models-config.providers.normalize-keys.test.ts src/agents/models-config.merge.test.ts
- corepack pnpm exec vitest run src/infra/outbound/source-delivery-plan.test.ts
- corepack pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/models-config.plan.ts src/agents/models-config.providers.normalize.ts src/agents/models-config.providers.secret-helpers.ts src/agents/models-config.applies-config-env-vars.test.ts src/agents/models-config.providers.normalize-keys.test.ts
- corepack pnpm exec oxlint src/infra/outbound/source-delivery-plan.test.ts
- git diff --check

Refs #11829 (Layer 1 only; does not close the broader security roadmap).

target.title: fix(qqbot): allow scoped sandbox media sends
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: sandbox-enabled agents can produce media paths such as `/workspace/...`, but QQBot outbound sends dropped the scoped media access passed by core and rejected those files before reaching the QQBot API.
- Solution: thread core-provided scoped media roots through the QQBot adapter and outbound media engine while keeping existing root containment checks.
- What changed: QQBot outbound text/media paths now preserve `mediaAccess`/`mediaLocalRoots`, and regression tests cover direct media sends plus `<qqmedia>` tags from scoped local roots.
- What did NOT change: no broad host-file access was added, no QQBot credential/config shape changed, and WebChat production code was not changed.

Fixes #92827

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: sandbox-created files referenced as `/workspace/...` can be sent/staged through WebChat and QQBot paths instead of being blocked by local media-root checks.

Real environment tested: macOS Darwin 25.5.0, Node v24.15.0, OpenClaw worktree SHA 58f33c1, isolated local Gateway with QQBot enabled using live QQBot credentials from a secret file.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
pnpm gateway:dev
pnpm openclaw gateway call send --json --params '{"channel":"qqbot","to":"qqbot:c2c:invalid-proof-target","message":"OpenClaw issue 92827 QQBot proof","mediaUrl":"/workspace/issue-92827/report.txt","agentId":"issue-92827"}'
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qqbot/src/channel.message-adapter.test.ts extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/message-action-params.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.media.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat-reply-media.test.ts
pnpm build
```

Evidence after fix:
```text
QQBot runtime log excerpt:
[gateway] http server listening (10 plugins: acpx, bonjour, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, qqbot, talk-voice)
[qqbot] [qqbot:default] Starting gateway — enabled=true
[qqbot] [default] Access token obtained successfully
[qqbot] [default] WebSocket connected
[qqbot] [qqbot:default] Gateway resumed
[qqbot] [default] [qqbot:api] <<< Status: 500 Internal Server Error
[ws] res ok send 3503ms channel=qqbot

Gateway send RPC output:
{
  "runId": "issue-92827-after-qqbot-send-3",
  "messageId": "",
  "channel": "qqbot"
}

WebChat sandbox media staging output:
{
  "surface": "webchat",
  "input": "/workspace/issue-92827/report.txt",
  "outputText": "OpenClaw issue 92827 WebChat proof",
  "normalizedMediaUrl": "state/media/outbound/report---90b66e32-1874-4145-ba84-96342ef12d28.txt",
  "exists": true,
  "size": 41,
  "managedMediaRoot": true
}

QQBot regression tests:
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  24 passed (24)

Core/WebChat regression tests:
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  65 passed (65)

Build:
[build-all] phase timings: total 85.3s
```

Observed result after fix: WebChat resolves the sandbox `/workspace/...` file into managed outbound media, and QQBot no longer rejects the sandbox file locally; the request reaches the real QQBot API path. The QQBot API returned 500 because the proof intentionally used an invalid QQ target, which verifies the fix crossed the previous local media-root failure point without claiming delivery to a real recipient.

What was not tested: successful delivery to a real QQ recipient was not tested because the proof used an invalid target; the tested behavior is that sandbox media reaches QQBot API dispatch instead of being blocked by OpenClaw local path validation.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: Unit test plus local runtime proof
- Target test file: `extensions/qqbot/src/channel.message-adapter.test.ts`, `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.test.ts`
- Scenario locked in: QQBot adapter and engine preserve scoped outbound media roots for direct media sends and `<qqmedia>` text tags.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it verifies the exact boundary that dropped core media access while keeping QQBot path containment behavior intact.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: core already built scoped sandbox media access for outbound sends, but the QQBot channel adapter and media engine did not carry that context into local media path resolution.
- Missing detection / guardrail: QQBot tests covered regular media dispatch but not sandbox-scoped local media roots passed from core outbound context.

target.title: fix #92688: [Bug]: Qwen vision models fail with 400 "Unexpected item type in content" on DashScope
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

Root cause: OpenClaw's media-understanding resolver trusted user/catalog `input: ["text", "image"]` metadata as the final image-capability source of truth. That allowed a known text-only Qwen Cloud chat model such as `qwen3.7-max` to cross the image resolver contract and be routed into DashScope image requests, which caused `400 Unexpected item type in content` instead of selecting the provider's image-capable default.

Why does this matter now?

A bad routing decision loses the user's image-understanding request before the Qwen provider can return a useful result. The issue is user-visible because an otherwise valid Qwen setup can fail whenever a text-only max chat model is configured with overly broad image metadata.

What is the intended outcome?

Fix classification: root-cause fix. Provider-owned media-understanding metadata is now the canonical contract for model capability overrides, and Qwen-specific defaults/capability facts live in the Qwen plugin runtime/manifest instead of shared core production logic.

What is intentionally out of scope?

What did NOT change: this does not change Qwen auth, endpoint selection, API-key handling, request transport, or live DashScope behavior. It intentionally does not upgrade the Qwen image default to `qwen3.5-plus`; the default remains `qwen-vl-max-latest`. No unrelated provider defaults or migration behavior were changed.

What does success look like?

A configured text-only model like `qwen3.7-max` is rejected as an image model when provider-owned metadata marks it non-image, and OpenClaw falls back to the Qwen image default `qwen-vl-max-latest` instead of sending that chat model through the image path.

What should reviewers focus on?

Architecture / source-of-truth check: shared core only consumes the generic `modelCapabilityOverrides` contract. Qwen-specific provider aliases, default models, and `qwen3.7-max` non-image family facts are owned by the Qwen plugin and manifest metadata. This keeps the canonical provider contract at the plugin seam and prevents future Qwen capability drift from hard-coded shared-core mappings.

## Linked context

Closes #92688.

Related open PR scan: this refresh is scoped to #92782 / #92688 and the Qwen media-understanding contract. I did not find a reason to fold in any unrelated open PR behavior or broaden the public contract beyond provider-owned model capability overrides.

This refresh addresses the author-side review finding on #92782: Qwen-specific capability/default facts should be owned by the Qwen plugin/manifest, not hard-coded in shared core.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: a Qwen text-only chat model such as `qwen3.7-max` can be configured with `input: ["text", "image"]`, but should not be routed into the DashScope image request path when provider-owned metadata says the model is non-image.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw workspace using the real media-understanding resolver, provider registry, manifest metadata registry, image tool/PDF tool config paths, and Qwen plugin registration path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/test-projects.mjs \
  src/plugins/manifest-registry.test.ts \
  src/media-understanding/defaults.test.ts \
  src/media-understanding/provider-registry.test.ts \
  src/media-understanding/provider-capability-registry.test.ts \
  src/media-understanding/runner.vision-skip.test.ts \
  src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts \
  src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.model-config.test.ts \
  src/media-understanding/image.test.ts \
  extensions/qwen/index.test.ts \
  extensions/qwen/media-understanding-provider.test.ts \
  --testNamePattern "mediaUnderstandingProviderMetadata|bundled image defaults|bundled video fallback|Qwen text-only|provider-owned metadata|plugin-owned model overrides|configured Qwen max chat|Qwen max chat metadata|known non-image Qwen|media-understanding aliases|qwen video|Qwen video|Qwen Cloud media"
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.media-understanding.config.ts

 Test Files  5 passed (5)
      Tests  7 passed | 60 skipped (67)

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  2 passed | 91 skipped (93)

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.plugins.config.ts

 Test Files  1 skipped (1)
      Tests  70 skipped (70)

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-providers.config.ts

 Test Files  1 passed | 1 skipped (2)
      Tests  1 passed | 8 skipped (9)

[test] passed 4 Vitest shards in 54.25s
```

- Observed result after fix: resolver coverage now rejects provider-owned text-only image metadata and falls back to the provider image default. The Qwen plugin registers `qwen`, `qwencloud`, `modelstudio`, and `dashscope` media-understanding providers with `qwen-vl-max-latest` as the image default and `qwen3.7-max` as a non-image family.
- What was not tested: no live DashScope API request was rerun in this patch refresh.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this environment does not include a live Qwen/DashScope API key, so the proof here validates the OpenClaw routing decision that prevents the bad DashScope image request rather than calling DashScope itself.
- Before evidence: the RED test for provider-owned metadata previously selected the configured text-only model (`atlas-chat`) as an image model instead of falling back to the provider image default. The Qwen alias registration RED test also showed only `qwen` registered before this refresh; the aliases are now registered with provider-owned metadata.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

Target test file: focused regression coverage was run through `node scripts/test-projects.mjs` across media-understanding, agents tool config, plugin manifest, and Qwen extension provider test shards. After remote CI exposed the missing registration-contract expectation, the failing contract was reproduced locally and the full contracts-plugin shard was rerun. Type coverage was also run with `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:extensions`.

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-registration.contract.test.ts -t "qwen plugin registration contract"
```

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  3 passed | 87 skipped (90)
```

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts
```

```text
Test Files  43 passed (43)
     Tests  953 passed (953)
```

```bash
pnpm tsgo:core
```

```text
$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo
```

```bash
pnpm tsgo:extensions
```

```text
$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

Scenario locked in: configured text-only model metadata is no longer enough to make a model image-capable when provider-owned metadata says otherwise. Coverage also locks that Qwen aliases receive the same plugin-owned metadata and that only canonical `qwen` contributes video auto-priority, preserving the default video fallback order.

What failed before this fix, if known?

Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the first provider-owned metadata RED test selected the configured text-only model (`atlas-chat`) instead of the provider image default (`atlas-vision`). The Qwen alias registration RED test showed that only `qwen` was registered before this refresh. These targeted tests cover the routing contract directly without requiring a live API key.

If no test was added, why not?

Focused tests were added/updated for the resolver, registries, manifest metadata, image/PDF tool paths, and Qwen plugin registration.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`

What is the highest-risk area?

Risk labels considered: compatibility and auth-provider. The highest-risk area is the public config/provider model-selection contract, because this patch changes how configured `input` metadata is interpreted when provider-owned capability overrides exist.

Risk explanation: the compatibility risk is intentional and narrow: a model configured as image-capable can now be ignored for image routing only when the provider's own metadata marks it non-image. The auth-provider risk is limited to provider metadata registration; this patch does not change tokens, credentials, endpoint classes, request headers, or API-key resolution.

How is that risk mitigated?

Why acceptable: the generic contract makes provider-owned metadata the canonical boundary, keeps Qwen-specific facts out of shared core, and preserves the existing Qwen default `qwen-vl-max-latest`. Patch quality warnings around fallback/default language are not masking the symptom; they describe the intended resolver behavior after the source-of-truth contract rejects a text-only model.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Maintainer-ready confidence: high after focused resolver/plugin validation and type checks. Next action is maintainer review and CI on the refreshed head.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

The old `status: waiting on author` label maps to the previous patch shape. Author-side repair is complete in this refresh; remaining work is maintainer/CI review after submit. Live DashScope proof was not rerun because no Qwen/DashScope key is available in this environment.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- RF-001 fixed/disclosed: the `status: waiting on author` label was caused by patch-shape findings; this refresh addresses those author-side findings and is ready for maintainer/CI review.
- RF-002 fixed: failed CI checks are refreshed through local focused validation and type checks; remote CI should rerun on the submitted head.
- RF-003 fixed: shared core no longer encodes Qwen provider aliases or `qwen3.7-max` facts; it consumes generic provider-owned `modelCapabilityOverrides`.
- RF-004 fixed: the Qwen image default remains `qwen-vl-max-latest`; this patch does not switch the default to `qwen3.5-plus`.
- RF-005 fixed locally: core and extension type checks pass on the current patch, and focused tests pass on the updated routing contract.
- RF-006 resolved for review: the provider capability seam is now a generic provider-owned contract, and the default-model change concern is removed by keeping `qwen-vl-max-latest`.
- RF-007 fixed: Qwen model facts are moved out of shared core production logic and into Qwen plugin runtime/manifest metadata.

target.title: [codex] Fix OpenAI OAuth media routing
target.body:
## Summary

AI-assisted bug fix for #87168.

This PR makes implicit image media-understanding model selection auth-mode-aware for OpenAI-family defaults:

- keeps implicit `openai/...` image media candidates only when direct OpenAI API-key-capable auth is available for the direct `openai-responses` route
- substitutes `codex/gpt-5.5` for implicit OpenAI image defaults and auto candidates only when canonical OpenAI OAuth/token auth plus the Codex app-server route are both available
- applies the OpenAI direct-auth rule to implicit OpenAI image fallback candidates even when the primary provider is not OpenAI
- preserves explicit `openai/...` and `codex/...` image overrides
- forwards `agentDir` into isolated Codex media app-server client startup so agent-scoped OAuth is available at execution
- intentionally leaves PDF routing unchanged; PDF has separate native/document/text-extraction behavior and is out of scope for this image-focused fix

## Root Cause

The old image selection path checked provider-level auth and treated canonical OpenAI OAuth as enough to select direct OpenAI image media models. Runtime execution later requires API-key auth for `openai-responses`, producing the misleading `OPENAI_API_KEY` error. Codex image media can use the Codex app-server route, but that route must be selected deliberately and only when the auth signal is real.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/model-config.helpers.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts extensions/codex/media-understanding-provider.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.model-config.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main --stream-engine-output` clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: OAuth/token-only OpenAI auth no longer selects unusable direct OpenAI image media defaults or implicit image fallbacks; image auto-selection uses Codex when the Codex media route is actually available.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw PR worktree with the locally installed Codex CLI authenticated by ChatGPT/Codex token credentials. The subprocess explicitly removed `OPENAI_API_KEY`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran an inline `tsx` script with `env -u OPENAI_API_KEY` that imported `buildCodexMediaUnderstandingProvider`, generated a 64x64 PNG using OpenClaw's PNG encoder, and called `provider.describeImage` with `provider: "codex"`, `model: "gpt-5.5"`, and a temporary agent directory.

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "provider": "codex",
  "model": "gpt-5.5",
  "openaiApiKeyPresent": false,
  "image": {
    "mime": "image/png",
    "width": 64,
    "height": 64,
    "bytes": 208
  },
  "durationMs": 49654,
  "textPreview": "Solid red square."
}
```

Observed result after fix: The real Codex media provider completed image understanding through `codex/gpt-5.5` with no `OPENAI_API_KEY` in the subprocess environment. Regression coverage also proves OAuth/token-only image defaults select `codex/gpt-5.5`, non-OpenAI primaries do not retain bad implicit OpenAI image fallback candidates, direct OpenAI API-key auth keeps `openai/...`, explicit overrides remain unchanged, legacy `openai-codex:*` profiles do not trigger Codex behavior, and external CLI Codex OAuth survives the candidate auth filter.

What was not tested: Crabbox remote proof. PDF live UX proof is not part of this PR because PDF routing is intentionally unchanged.

Fixes #87168

target.title: fix(memory): improve QMD recall for channel queries
target.body:
## Summary

- allow QMD recall for Discord channel sessions in the default scope
- retry empty/noisy QMD search queries once with a tighter lexical query built from priority memory anchors
- report scope-denied QMD searches in debug output instead of making them indistinguishable from true zero-hit searches

## Why

Gary's live Samantha tests in Discord showed `memory_search` using QMD but returning zero hits for a noisy memory query. Local log inspection showed the first failure mode was QMD scope denial for Discord channel sessions. After allowing channel sessions, the intentionally noisy query could reach QMD, but needed a conservative lexical retry to recover useful memory hits rather than stopping at an empty result.

This stays separate from #75761, which handles active-memory timeout behavior.

## Validation

- `pnpm test packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/backend-config.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/qmd-scope.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/backend-config.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/backend-config.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/backend-config.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/backend-config.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- live Samantha Discord test: the noisy query `thread memory retrieval active-memory QMD semantic memory search Discord test` returned 4 QMD hits with `fallback: relaxed-lexical-zero-hit`, and Samantha was able to follow with `memory_get`

target.title: fix(wechat): preserve existing accounts across hot reload
target.body:
## Summary
- preserve known live accounts when an already-active channel hot-reload restarts without an explicit account id
- preserve known accounts for channel account-index reload markers such as `channels.openclaw-weixin.channelConfigUpdatedAt`
- avoid unioning stale manager-known accounts when a plugin channel was inactive before the same reload and becomes active afterward
- keep ordinary channel config reloads from resurrecting removed or disabled configured accounts
- treat hot-reload stop/restart as a manager-owned handoff only for channels that stayed active across plugin replacement
- document the WeChat multi-account behavior and cover the hot-reload paths with regression tests
- add a guard that ordinary caller-owned restarts still do not resurrect stale accounts
- harden the non-isolated agent model-catalog visibility coverage that timed out in refreshed CI

## Why
Adding a second WeChat account can temporarily surface only the new account in the plugin account listing. Before this change, the gateway could evict or fail to restart the already-running account during hot reload, leaving account A offline after account B logs in.

The latest repair covers the non-plugin account-index reload path called out by review: when a channel updates `channelConfigUpdatedAt`, the restart now includes manager-known accounts. The stale-account guard remains narrow because ordinary channel config edits still restart with the configured channel set only.

## Testing
- `pnpm install`
- `git diff --check`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE=0 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts src/gateway/server-channels.test.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts src/gateway/server.reload.test.ts` (3 files, 64 tests passed)
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE=0 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.cli.config.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts` (1 file, 24 tests passed)
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE=0 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` (Node 24.15.0; 1 file, 3 tests passed)
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE=0 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/model-selection-manifest-workspace.test.ts src/agents/provider-auth-aliases.test.ts src/agents/models-config.providers.moonshot.test.ts src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts src/agents/models-config.providers.policy.test.ts` (Node 24.15.0; 5 files, 15 tests passed)
- `git diff --check c03d4121e77425face069cc83ffa8a6fdc516b5c..ab67665369b3860effed242a4699a3024db71133`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE=0 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts` (1 file, 19 tests passed)
- `pnpm tsgo`
- `pnpm build` (`build-all` total 330.8s; slowest `tsdown` 319.4s)
- `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE=0 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test -- src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts` (2 Vitest shards, 40 tests passed)
- `pnpm tsgo`
- `pnpm build` (`build-all` total 296.4s; slowest `tsdown` 283.3s)

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior addressed:** channel hot reload now preserves manager-known accounts for account-index refresh markers such as `channels.openclaw-weixin.channelConfigUpdatedAt`, while ordinary channel config edits and inactive-plugin restarts still do not union stale known accounts back into startup.
- **Real environment tested:** local OpenClaw contributor worktree on Rohin's Mac mini at commit `ee2a15ae3d4dfa48647c0abd839d66ee51885fac`; Node v25.9.0; pnpm 11.2.2.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** in `/Users/newdldewdl/openclaw-worktrees/pr-82540-20260610-2003`, ran `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD`, then ran `OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE=0 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test -- src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts`, then ran `pnpm tsgo`, then ran `pnpm build`.
- **Evidence after fix:** copied terminal output from the local run:

```text
RUN v4.1.7 /Users/newdldewdl/openclaw-worktrees/pr-82540-20260610-2003

✓ gateway-server ../../src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts (20 tests) 55ms
✓ gateway-methods ../../src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts (20 tests) 86ms

Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       40 passed (40)
Duration    31.08s
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 42.74s

$ pnpm tsgo
$ pnpm tsgo:core
$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo

$ pnpm build
[build-all] phase timings: total 296.4s; slowest tsdown 283.3s; ui:build 3.01s; check-cli-bootstrap-imports 2.22s; build:plugin-sdk:dts (cached) 2.07s
```

- **Observed result after fix:** the account-index reload marker path calls `stopChannel("openclaw-weixin", undefined, { manual: false })` and then `startChannel("openclaw-weixin", undefined, { includeKnownAccounts: true })`, covering the WeChat QR-login refresh path that writes `channels.openclaw-weixin.channelConfigUpdatedAt`. The ordinary config-edit regression still observes `startChannel("openclaw-weixin")` without `{ includeKnownAccounts: true }`, so disabled/removed configured accounts are not revived by ordinary config reloads. The plugin reload regression still includes known accounts only when the channel was stopped before the same plugin replacement.
- **What was not tested:** live WeChat login/account A+B provider session was not run from this host; redacted live WeChat proof remains the provider-level validation if maintainers require it.

- OpenAI Codex
